_meta.note: Single content source for portfolio-shwetabh.lexnovahq.com. Stripped/watermarked-safe: no commercial framing, no Architect Notes, no secrets. Matrix rows derived from verified LEX_NOVA_REGISTRY_v2 (98 threats).
_meta.page_title: Shwetabh Singh — a litigator making AI legal work defensible enough to deploy
_meta.og_title: Shwetabh Singh — a litigator making AI legal work defensible enough to deploy
_meta.og_description: Doctrine → Taxonomy → Instruments → Tooling → Systems. The path the work took, from the law itself to the systems that run it.

hero.name: Shwetabh Singh
hero.identity_line: Technology & Data Protection Lawyer | AI Governance & Cross-Border Privacy (GDPR · US State Laws · DPDP) | Commercial Contracts for Startups & Tech
hero.spine_explainer: The path the work actually took — from the law itself to the systems that run it.
hero.hook: I practised litigation for three and a half years. Then I built systems that do the substantive part of the work — diagnosing risk, drafting the defenses, running the diligence — and proved them on AI law.
hero.thesis: The hard part of legal AI isn't the AI — it's making a probabilistic machine deterministic enough to trust with law. That's what I engineered.
hero.proof_line: Everything here is live, open, or on video. Run it, read the code, watch it work.
hero.credential_bar: Three and a half years in litigation (Patna High Court) · B.A. LL.B., NLU Odisha · building it hands-on since October 2025

act1.orientation_line: This portfolio runs in two halves. Acts I–II are the law — every AI risk mapped, and the clauses that close it. Act III is the systems I built to do that work automatically. The systems exist to deliver the law.
act1.header: Foundation — every AI legal risk, mapped.
act1.forward_ref: → what the diligence engine later detects on its own.
act1.intro: Each one stays close to the real legal problem — what creates the exposure, what closes it, what holds up. I built them in AI law: contracts, compliance, privacy, AI liability, diligence, digital business risk. The build is just the delivery — the legal judgment is the point.

reference_layer.frame: The reference layer the systems read from.
reference_layer.worked_line: A model is probabilistic (mechanism) → its confident false output creates reliance liability (consequence) → a hallucination-specific waiver + output-authority boundary closes it (remediation).
reference_layer.scope_stat: 98 threats · 10 archetypes · 10 surfaces · 3 jurisdictions — authored solo since October 2025, refined from 81 through forensic-engine runs.
reference_layer.applied_practice: Applied-practice layer shown frosted, with a count only. No raw corpus, no commercial labels.

risk_map.note: Classify → trigger. Select an archetype → its surfaces light over the Global Floor → click a lit surface → modal pulls the matching registry rows (authority IN/EU/US + clause). Preset-driven entry; on-hover definitions on every archetype and surface.

global_floor.label: Global Floor (Universal)
global_floor.def: The baseline every AI product is bound by, regardless of what it does — consent, transparency, third-party pass-throughs.

archetypes.name: Doer
archetypes.def: Executes tasks or spends money autonomously (AI SDRs, automated support, agents).

archetypes.name: Judge
archetypes.def: Scores or screens humans for opportunity (HR screeners, loan approvals).

archetypes.name: Companion
archetypes.def: Sustains emotional relationships or roleplay (virtual therapists, conversational agents).

archetypes.name: Creator
archetypes.def: Generates net-new media or code (image generators, voice cloners, copilots).

archetypes.name: Reader
archetypes.def: Consumes and vectorizes external data (RAG pipelines, scrapers).

archetypes.name: Orchestrator
archetypes.def: Routes prompts to third-party models (API gateways, model routers).

archetypes.name: Translator
archetypes.def: Bridges formats, often via physical inputs (meeting note-takers, speech-to-text).

archetypes.name: Shield
archetypes.def: Monitors or audits enterprise environments (AI pen-testing, compliance bots).

archetypes.name: Optimizer
archetypes.def: Manages critical or financial infrastructure (algorithmic trading, grid control).

archetypes.name: Mover
archetypes.def: Acts on the physical world (warehouse robotics, autonomous drones).

surfaces.name: PII
surfaces.def: Processing identifiable personal data — names, emails, IPs, user text.

surfaces.name: Sensitive/Biometric
surfaces.def: Health, voice, or facial-geometry data — triggers strict biometric regimes.

surfaces.name: Financial
surfaces.def: AI managing trades, credit, or payments.

surfaces.name: Minors
surfaces.def: Users under 18 — triggers age-design and parental-consent regimes.

surfaces.name: Employment
surfaces.def: Screening candidates or employees — triggers bias-audit mandates.

surfaces.name: Consumer-Public
surfaces.def: Mass-market B2C exposure — dark-patterns, takedown, class-action surface.

surfaces.name: Enterprise-Private
surfaces.def: B2B SaaS to large organizations — SLA and wasted-expenditure exposure.

surfaces.name: Infrastructure
surfaces.def: Reliance on foundation-model APIs or critical infrastructure.

surfaces.name: Content&IP
surfaces.def: Generated media, training data, and ownership of output.

risk_map.diagnostic_format: Mechanical trigger → Law (IN / US / EU) → Remediation

law.eu: GDPR Art. 22 + AI Act Annex III (high-risk; 2 Dec 2027)
law.us: Title VII / EEOC · NYC LL144 · IL HB3773 · TX TRAIGA

case_law.name: Moffatt v. Air Canada
case_law.line: The chatbot speaks with the company's authority; no “separate tool” defence.

case_law.name: Thaler v. Perlmutter
case_law.line: No copyright for autonomous AI output; forces human-in-the-loop.

case_law.name: Bartz v. Anthropic
case_law.line: The training-data reckoning; forces upstream IP indemnities.

case_law.name: Mobley v. Workday
case_law.line: AI hiring tools face disparate-impact liability.

case_law.name: FTC v. Rite Aid
case_law.line: Algorithmic disgorgement; a regulator can force the model destroyed.

case_law.name: Cruz v. Fireflies
case_law.line: Meeting-capture AI triggers strict biometric / recording consent.

act1.connector1: A map of every AI risk protects no one on its own. It matters only once it becomes something a company can deploy — so I turned the doctrine into instruments.

act2.header: Act II — The Legal Matrix: the clauses that close those risks.
act2.forward_ref: → what the Vault later assembles automatically.
act2.system_framing: A classification system I built and refined — 98 threats, role-coded, read across three jurisdictions.
act2.provenance: 98 active threats, refined from 81 through forensic-engine runs; schema normalized for cross-border coverage.

laneA.line: Master terms; routes every other document by trigger.
Data processing terms; fires on PII / EU data.
Agent addendum; fires on autonomous capability.

Prompt-assignment + ownership chain for internal output.
Data-protection impact assessment for internal tools.

living_document.behavior: Real TOS (the spine doc) renders fully blurred at rest. Click a button → scroll + pop + un-blur the specific clause, with the 3-beat explanation. Full animation. Mobile degrades to stacked full-screen sequence.

threat_buttons.what: A hallucination-specific waiver + output-authority boundary + point-of-interaction clickwrap.
threat_buttons.why: Moffatt binds the chatbot's words to the company; a point-of-interaction disclaimer defeats the reasonable reliance the company would otherwise owe (Walters). A generic “AS-IS” clause does neither.

threat_buttons.what: Per-incident spend cap, retry and loop limits, and machine-action attribution.
threat_buttons.why: UETA §14 attributes an electronic agent's actions to the principal — so the agent's spending is the company's liability. The circuit breaker caps the blast radius before it compounds.

threat_buttons.what: Mandates retrieval-augmented generation over fine-tuning, so user data never bakes into model weights.
threat_buttons.why: Fine-tuning makes data mathematically un-deletable; an erasure request then can't be honored, exposing FTC algorithmic disgorgement (Rite Aid) and breach of GDPR Art. 17 / DPDP §8(7). RAG keeps deletion possible.

threat_buttons.threat: Your AI output is public domain
threat_buttons.what: Assigns the human selection/arrangement logic and mandates human-in-the-loop authorship.
threat_buttons.why: Raw autonomous AI output isn't copyrightable (Thaler), so a standard “assign all copyrights” transfers nothing. The chain secures enterprise IP by anchoring it to human authorship.

threat_buttons.threat: AI reclassified as a product (strict liability)
threat_buttons.what: Defines the offering as a service, not a product, with the supporting structural terms.
threat_buttons.why: The revised EU Product Liability Directive can sweep software/AI into strict product liability. The classification clause holds the service line and the defenses that go with it.

threat_buttons.what: Layered consent and lawful-basis architecture across three regimes.
threat_buttons.why: Consent valid in one jurisdiction is invalid in another: DPDP §6 notice+consent, GDPR Art. 6–7 lawful basis, and CCPA opt-out are different instruments. The clause layers all three.

trigger_buttons.note: Wired to the real vault.js engine and the Act I surfaces. Click a condition → the router fires → connected docs pop alongside, hooks lit → labelled ‘what the Vault injects automatically when you flag this →’ (hands into Act III).

items.fires: RAG → DPA §4 fires; Fine-tune → §4 omitted, TOS §6.1(c) opt-in

living_document.vault_tease: B2C + EU data → Consumer + EU surfaces → injects §8 and attaches the DPA into Schedule A → this is the base the Vault assembles from →

act2.connector2: But a document stack is still hand-work: someone reading the registry, choosing the clauses, assembling the file. The leverage was never the document — it was the system that produces it. So I built that.

act3.header: Act III — The Funnel: the machine that runs it.
act3.scope_note: Live tools (the four sandboxes) and the videos are a separate build phase. Structure, copy, the OS map, the automation-library link, and Connector 3 are real now.
act3.determinism_principle: Here's why you can trust the funnel: the registry constrains the model, the Vault→Assembly is deterministic, and the residual judgment goes to human review. As deterministic as law requires.

master_video.frame: Primary exhibit — a chaptered full-power walkthrough of the project-version engines (most powerful models, in the ChatGPT/Claude project interface), narrated for the prompt/agent engineering. Chapters: Signal → Diligence → Assembly → CRM. Shows the client-deliverable docs (Architect Notes excluded) and the threat-registry CSV as provenance. CRM chapter uses synthetic data only.

act3.os_map: One synthetic record traced end-to-end through the stations.

stations.name: Signal
stations.frame: Engineering proof, not a product — multi-agent LLM pipeline.

stations.name: Diligence
stations.frame: Run a URL or upload a doc → Risk Report (mechanical trigger → law IN/US/EU → remediation). AI-risk diligence; the same engine generalises to any diligence domain.

stations.name: Assembly
stations.frame: One closed-loop engine, step-by-step, synthetic input. Law-as-code. Manage = synthetic sandbox, never the real portal.

stations.name: Lane B Scanner
stations.frame: The deploy-side counterpart — the key Lane B artifact.

stations.name: Automation Engine
stations.frame: Public, audited repo (real now) + a runnable document pipeline: drop a doc → OCR → extract → classify → summarise → route. The same architecture, generalised.

act3.connector3: Every system here runs on one spine — the scanner reads the registry, the Vault assembles the clauses, and the platform proves the architecture isn't specific to law at all. Which is the whole point.

capstone.title: One Mind: The Legal Problem First — Across Three Jurisdictions

crossborder.in: IT Act 2000 → DPDP Act 2023 · CPA 2019 · Copyright Act 1957 · IT Amendment Rules 2026 (3-hour takedown) · MeitY advisories
crossborder.eu: GDPR · EU AI Act 2024/1689 (Art. 50: 2 Aug 2026; high-risk Annex III: 2 Dec 2027) · Product Liability Directive 2024/2853
crossborder.us: UCC Art. 2/2A · UETA §14 · CCPA/CPRA · TX TRAIGA · IL HB3773 (Colorado AI Act: repealed/replaced — not active)

parallel_map.header: The same legal discipline, even in the engineering.
parallel_map.frame: The law came first and trained the instinct — classification, dependency, controlled assembly — and it shows up when I build software. Not a method that generalises across domains; a legal mind that shaped the code.

capstone.identity_line: A litigator who taught himself to build — not a builder who picked up law. The legal judgment leads; the code is only how I ship it.

close.body: I'm a lawyer by instinct, not by accident. I spent three and a half years in a courtroom before I wrote a line of code, and what drew me next wasn't technology for its own sake — it was the place where law and technology collide. Privacy, data protection, AI: the questions there are still open, the old answers don't quite fit, and that's exactly what pulled me in. So I taught myself to build — not to leave the law, but to go deeper into it than paper alone would let me. The technology made me a sharper lawyer; it was never the other way round. I didn't become a builder who picked up law. I stayed a lawyer who learned to build. I'd rather do this work — at the edge of tech and privacy law — inside a team already taking it seriously.
close.governance_note: Models are routed by data sensitivity. Public-only diagnostics run on free fast-inference tiers — no privacy cost, the input is already public. Client and work data run on paid API tiers with model training disabled. Every demo here uses public or synthetic data only.
close.genuineness: All work shown is mine; every link and demo is genuine.
